items.title: Local models are reviewers, planners and proposers only — never unguarded code writers
items.description: A local model may review code, plan work, or propose a catalogue, but it must never write committed code without a deterministic stage between its output and the repository. Its text is always treated as a suggestion to be validated, not as source to be shipped.
items.applicability: Every place a local model is consulted in LocalAIFactory.
items.example: A model flags a missing null-check and proposes a patch; a human or a deterministic template applies the actual fix, and the model's text is never committed verbatim.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: route model output to a review or proposal channel, never to a file writer. Test implication: assert no code path lets raw model text reach a committed file. Generator implication: proposals feed the governed catalogue, not the emitter directly. Boundary: this constrains how output is used, not the quality of the model's reasoning.

items.title: Keep an explicit model-role roster
items.description: Each local model is pinned to a role: qwen2.5-coder:14b as code reviewer and proposer, deepseek-r1:14b as planner and reasoning reviewer, and nomic-embed-text as optional embeddings. The roster is explicit so the router never sends a planning prompt to an embedding model or a code prompt to a planner by accident.
items.applicability: Configuring which model the router selects for a given task type.
items.example: A 'review this diff' task routes to qwen2.5-coder; a 'plan the migration steps' task routes to deepseek-r1; an embedding request routes to nomic-embed-text.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store the roster as config, not as scattered string literals. Test implication: each task type resolves to the expected role. Generator implication: role assignment is data the router reads. Boundary: the roster reflects observed suitability for this estate, not a universal ranking of these models.

items.title: Give deepseek-r1 a larger num_predict because it spends tokens on hidden reasoning
items.description: deepseek-r1 is a reasoning model that emits hidden <think> output before its answer. With a small num_predict it can exhaust the budget inside the thinking phase and return empty. Its requests need a deliberately larger num_predict so the visible answer survives after the reasoning tokens.
items.applicability: Any router call that targets deepseek-r1 for planning or reasoning review.
items.example: A planning prompt to deepseek-r1 with num_predict 256 returns blank; raising num_predict so the budget covers the <think> span and the answer produces a usable plan.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: set per-model token budgets, with a higher floor for reasoning models. Test implication: a reasoning call returns non-empty visible output under the configured budget. Generator implication: budget is part of the model role config. Boundary: a larger budget raises latency and memory, so it is scoped to the reasoning role only.

items.title: Route by role, not by hard-coded model name at the call site
items.description: Callers ask the router for a role (code-review, planning, embedding) and the router resolves the concrete model. Call sites never name a model directly, so swapping or upgrading a model is a single config change, not a sweep across the codebase.
items.applicability: All internal consumers of local inference.
items.example: A controller requests the 'planner' role; the router maps it to deepseek-r1 today and could map it to a different model tomorrow without touching the controller.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: expose role-based methods on the router and keep model ids inside it. Test implication: a role request resolves to the configured model and changing the mapping needs no call-site edit. Generator implication: role indirection is the public surface. Boundary: indirection helps maintainability but cannot make an unsuitable model good at a role.

items.title: Health-probe the model host with /api/tags before routing
items.description: Before sending work, the router probes the Ollama host (for example /api/tags) to learn whether the service is up and which models are present. The probe result is cached so the request path reads a snapshot rather than hitting the host synchronously per call.
items.applicability: Deciding whether a role can be served locally right now.
items.example: The probe shows qwen2.5-coder is pulled but deepseek-r1 is not; planning requests degrade while code-review requests proceed.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: cache probe results on a timer, never block a controller on a live probe. Test implication: with a stubbed probe the router routes correctly and a missing model degrades cleanly. Generator implication: availability is read from the cache. Boundary: a cached probe can be briefly stale, so calls must still tolerate a model that vanished since the last probe.

items.title: Degrade gracefully when Ollama is absent — every call returns unavailable, never throws
items.description: When Ollama is missing, every router call returns a typed 'unavailable' result rather than throwing. The caller then falls back to deterministic reasoning. No request path is allowed to fail because a model is not installed.
items.applicability: MSSQL-only deployments and any environment with no local model host.
items.example: A review request on a model-less box returns 'unavailable'; the caller proceeds with deterministic checks and the page still renders.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: model the result as available/unavailable, not as throw-on-failure. Test implication: with the host down every role returns unavailable and no exception escapes. Generator implication: deterministic fallback is the default path. Boundary: graceful degradation preserves availability, not the enrichment the model would have added.

items.title: Core tests must pass without Ollama by injecting a fake or null client
items.description: The test suite must run green with no model host present by injecting a fake or null inference client. This keeps CI deterministic and proves the system does not secretly depend on a live model to function.
items.applicability: Unit and integration tests of any feature that can consult a model.
items.example: Tests register a fake router that returns canned 'unavailable' or fixed responses, so the suite runs identically on a CI agent with no Ollama.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: depend on a router interface so a fake can be injected. Test implication: the full suite passes with the fake client and no network. Generator implication: testability requires the seam to exist. Boundary: fake-client tests verify orchestration and fallback, not the behaviour of the real model.

items.title: Honour a prompt budget and a timeout on every model call
items.description: Each router call carries a prompt-size budget and a wall-clock timeout. A call that would exceed the prompt budget is trimmed or refused, and a call that exceeds the timeout is abandoned and treated as unavailable so a slow model never stalls a request.
items.applicability: All synchronous and background inference calls.
items.example: A review prompt that exceeds the budget drops lower-priority context to fit; a call that runs past its timeout returns unavailable and the caller falls back.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: pass a cancellation token plus a size cap into every call. Test implication: an over-budget prompt is trimmed or refused and a slow call times out and degrades. Generator implication: budgets and timeouts are per-role config. Boundary: trimming context can lower answer quality, which is the accepted cost of bounded latency.

items.title: Log every model call
items.description: Every router call logs the role, the resolved model, the outcome (answered, unavailable, timed out) and the elapsed time. This makes model usage observable and lets a hung or degraded model be located the same way a hung request is.
items.example: A log line records 'role=planner model=deepseek-r1 outcome=timeout 8200ms', flagging a model that needs attention.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: emit a structured log at the router boundary, not inside each caller. Test implication: a call produces exactly one outcome log line with the role and elapsed time. Generator implication: logging is centralised in the router. Boundary: logs aid diagnosis but are not a substitute for the prompt/response audit capture kept separately.

items.title: Capture the prompt and response to an audit log
items.description: Beyond outcome logging, the router captures the full prompt and the full response to an audit log so any model-influenced decision can be reconstructed and reviewed later. The audit record is the evidence trail behind a proposal or review.
items.applicability: Any model output that informs a code change, plan or proposal.
items.example: A review that prompted a fix is stored prompt-and-response so a later auditor sees exactly what the model was asked and what it answered.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: persist prompt and response to a dedicated audit store, separate from operational logs. Test implication: a call writes a retrievable audit record linking prompt to response. Generator implication: the audit trail backs provenance. Boundary: audit capture can hold sensitive prompt content, so the store must respect the project's no-secrets and data-handling rules.

items.title: Turn an accepted model finding into a code fix, test, knowledge item or backlog item
items.description: A model finding that is accepted must land as a concrete artefact: a code fix, a new test, a knowledge-pack item, or a tracked backlog entry. Findings that are merely read and forgotten provide no value and are not allowed to be the end state.
items.applicability: Triage of model review and planning output.
items.example: A model spots a missing maker-checker check; the team adds the guard, a negative test for it, and a knowledge item, closing the loop.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: route accepted findings into the existing fix/test/knowledge/backlog channels. Test implication: a sampled accepted finding maps to a committed artefact. Generator implication: accepted findings can become new knowledge items the generator later applies. Boundary: this governs accepted findings; rejected ones are logged with a reason, not actioned.

items.title: No model may bypass the safe tool gateway
items.description: Any action a model would take on the system goes through the sandboxed tool gateway with its command policy. The model proposes; the gateway decides and executes under policy. There is no direct path from model output to the filesystem, database or shell.
items.applicability: Wiring model suggestions to any side-effecting capability.
items.example: A model suggests running a migration; the request passes through the gateway policy, which permits or denies it, rather than the model invoking the command itself.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: there must be no code path that lets model text trigger a tool outside the gateway. Test implication: a model-proposed dangerous action is intercepted by the gateway policy. Generator implication: the gateway is the only execution surface. Boundary: the gateway enforces policy on actions, not the soundness of the model's intent behind them.

items.title: Treat model output as non-deterministic and govern it accordingly
items.description: The same prompt can yield different model answers, so no committed behaviour may depend on a specific model response. Model output is governed input: validated, bounded and reproducible only through the deterministic stages that consume it, never by replaying the model.
items.applicability: Reasoning about reproducibility wherever a model is in the loop.
items.example: Two runs of the same proposal prompt yield slightly different catalogues; both pass the same validation and produce identical committed code because the deterministic stage is what writes files.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: never assert on exact model text in a reproducibility check; assert on the validated, deterministic downstream. Test implication: reproduction tests target deterministic output, not raw model responses. Generator implication: non-determinism is isolated to the proposal stage. Boundary: this isolates non-determinism but does not remove it from the proposal itself.

items.title: Treat embeddings as an optional enrichment, not a hard dependency
items.description: nomic-embed-text embeddings enrich retrieval but are optional. When the embedding model or vector store is absent, retrieval falls back to deterministic non-vector paths and the system keeps working, consistent with Qdrant and Ollama being optional.
items.applicability: Retrieval and knowledge-injection paths that can use embeddings.
items.example: With no embedding model present, knowledge retrieval uses keyword and metadata filtering instead of vector similarity, and the page still loads.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: gate embedding use behind availability and provide a non-vector fallback. Test implication: retrieval returns sensible results with embeddings disabled. Generator implication: embedding is an enrichment role, not a baseline requirement. Boundary: the fallback preserves availability and basic relevance, not the ranking quality vector similarity would give.

items.title: Match the model role to the task, not the largest model to everything
items.description: Across evaluations, a code-tuned model reviewed code better while a reasoning model planned better; sending every task to one model wasted budget and produced weaker output. The router picks the role-appropriate model so each task gets the model that evaluated best for it.
items.applicability: Selecting a model for a mixed workload of review, planning and embedding.
items.example: Routing a diff review to qwen2.5-coder and a multi-step migration plan to deepseek-r1 produced better results than forcing both through a single model.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: encode task-to-role mapping rather than a single default model. Test implication: each task type resolves to its evaluated-best role. Generator implication: routing reflects measured suitability. Boundary: suitability was measured for this estate's tasks and model versions, so it should be re-evaluated when models change.
